interpretations.interpretation: A straightforward continuation of a sacred genealogy: the audience would understand it as listing successive ancestors through the traditional “X begat Y” formula, reinforcing that the account is intended as genealogical history rather than narrative or theology by itself.

A claim of legitimate ancestry for a messianic/royal figure: even though this specific snippet names only forebears, Matthew’s audience would likely connect such lines to the broader argument that Jesus’ identity is grounded in ancestral descent.

Fulfillment and continuity of God’s covenant history: many early Christian/Jewish readers would see genealogies as showing that God’s promises proceed through real generations and named people, demonstrating that the present story stands in continuity with Israel’s past.

Credibility through verifiable tradition: the use of specific names (Elioud, Eleazar, Matthan, Jacob) would be taken as part of an authoritative record that could, in principle, be recognized or checked within the community’s knowledge of family/tribal histories.

Preserving inherited identity and inheritance rights: an audience contemporary to the text would likely associate genealogical lines with matters of family standing, inheritance, and rightful place within the people of Israel.

A literary/stylistic sign of structured proof: the audience might read the repeated “begat” pattern as a form of cumulative argument—each clause adding one more link to an overall chain meant to support the surrounding claims of the passage.
